derivation: Read PNG: rows=WX, cols=YZ (Gray order 00,01,11,10), red cell indices confirm minterm numbering. 1s at m0,m2,m5,m7,m8,m10,m13,m15. Group {0,2,8,10}: X=0,Z=0, W/Y free -> X'Z'. Group {5,7,13,15}: X=1,Z=1, W/Y free -> XZ. F = XZ + X'Z' (X XNOR Z).
python3: 0b1001101 = 77 dec, 0x4d, 0o115. Verified by execution, not inference.

myAnswer: A: MOVE R1,R2 | B: MOVE #X,R1 | C: MOVE (A0),R1 | D: MOVE X(A0),R1
derivation: Open-ended. Register direct = operand is register contents (MOVE R1,R2); immediate = literal in the instruction (MOVE #X,R1); register indirect = register holds the address, operand at [A0]; indexed register indirect = displacement added to base register, operand at [[A0]+X]. All four shipped examples are valid 68000-style forms and match their mode definitions.
shippedAnswer: A. Move R1,R2; B. Move #X,R1; C. Move (A0),R1; D. Move X(A0),R1

myAnswer: Q = 1, 1, 0, 1 after the four rising edges (Q starts 0)
derivation: Read stem PNG: 4 clock rising edges. Sampling T only at those rising edges gives T = 1, 0, 1, 1. Positive-edge T-FF: toggle on T=1, hold on T=0. From Q=0: edge1 toggle->1, edge2 hold->1, edge3 toggle->0, edge4 toggle->1. Independently derived, then confirmed against the model-answer PNG, which shows Q rising at edge1, holding high past edge2, falling at edge3, rising at edge4.
shippedAnswer: See model-answer figure (Q drawn beneath Clock and T)

derivation: First standardized Ethernet (DIX / IEEE 802.3 10BASE5) ran at 10 Mbps. The 1973 Xerox PARC prototype was 2.94 Mbps but is not among the options; 100 Mbps is Fast Ethernet (1995). B = 10 Mbps.

DDS = Dataphone/Digital Data Service, a digital leased-line service. DDD (Direct Distance Dialing) and WATS (Wide Area Telephone Service) are analog switched-telephone services; DDT is not a telecom service. B.
Prim (and Kruskal, not offered) solve MST. Dijkstra minimizes source-to-node path cost, not total tree weight; Huffman builds prefix codes; DFS is a traversal. B. Unique correct option here.

Dijkstra is the canonical single-source shortest-path algorithm, so C is the intended key. BUT option A (Bellman-Ford) is ALSO a correct shortest-path algorithm (and strictly more general: handles negative edge weights). Two correct options = broken MCQ. Key intent C is still right; the item needs the Bellman-Ford distractor replaced.
int *p; declares a pointer (capital 'Int' is an OCR artifact). A (int &p) is a reference, not a pointer (and is ill-formed uninitialized); C is a plain value; 'Ptr' is not a C++ type. B.

D flip-flop latches its input on the clock edge, so Q(t+1) = D regardless of present state Q. Content of the key is correct and matches the PNG. DEFECT: the completed truth table is attached as stemFigure, i.e. the question displays its own answer. It should be answerFigure, not stemFigure.
A row in a relational table is a record (a.k.a. tuple). Field and column both name the vertical/attribute dimension; 'Entry' is not standard terminology for a row. D.
DROP TABLE (DDL) removes the table object, structure and catalog definition. DELETE (DML) removes rows only and leaves the empty table. A.

A Network Operating System is the software layer providing shared access to files/printers plus accounts and permissions. A router only forwards packets; IOS is one vendor's router OS. B. (Option D 'I don't remember' is a crowd-source artifact, already flagged hedged_option; does not affect the key.)

Different IPs = different subnets; joining distinct IP networks requires a layer-3 forwarding decision, which is the router's job. Switch/hub/bridge are layer 1-2 and only extend one broadcast domain. A.
